title: Totally nonparallel immersions
speaker: Michael Harrison
abstract: An immersion from a smooth n-dimensional manifold M into Rq is called totally nonparallel if, for every pair of distinct points x and y in M, the tangent spaces at f(x) and f(y) contain no parallel lines. The simplest example is the map R→R2 sending x to (x,x2). Given a manifold M, what is the minimum dimension q=q(M) such that M admits a totally nonparallel immersion into Rq?

proof_stages.label: Definition and elementary dimension bounds
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines a totally nonparallel immersion, introduces the minimal ambient dimension problem, records low-dimensional known values, and derives the basic lower bounds q ≥ 2n and q ≥ 2n+1 for compact manifolds together with the generic upper bound q ≤ 4n.

proof_stages.label: Quadratic construction from nonsingular symmetric bilinear maps
proof_stages.summary: Nonsingular symmetric bilinear maps are introduced as algebraic input, and the graph x ↦ (x,B(x,x)) is shown to be totally nonparallel, giving explicit Euclidean-space upper bounds such as R^n in R^{3n−1} or R^{3n−2} in the even-dimensional complex-polynomial case.

proof_stages.label: Configuration-space obstruction and main target theorem
proof_stages.summary: A totally nonparallel immersion f induces an immersion of the unordered configuration space through (x,y) ↦ f(x)+f(y), leading to obstruction theory, a converse h-principle question, projective-space lower bounds, and the announced universal upper bound in R^{4n−1}.

proof_stages.label: Haefliger component-replacement template for embeddings
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains the removal-of-singularities method in the embedding case: from an equivariant map with zero set the diagonal, replace components one at a time by differences f_i(x)−f_i(y), using the zero set of the remaining components and a projection-injectivity dimension count in the metastable range.

proof_stages.label: Near-diagonal control by skew bundle maps
proof_stages.summary: Because the required nonzero condition is not open near the diagonal, a diagonal neighborhood is identified with TM, the equivariant map becomes a skew bundle map, and Hirsch plus Smale-Hirsch theory allow it to be modified near the diagonal to look like the differential of an immersion before gluing to the off-diagonal construction.

proof_stages.label: Semifree local model for totally nonparallel maps
proof_stages.summary: For totally nonparallel immersions the coalescing-points limit is a second-order condition: the projected second derivative, equivalently the second fundamental form, must be a nonsingular symmetric bilinear map; generic maps M^n → R^{4n−1} satisfy this semifree condition and are locally totally nonparallel.

proof_stages.label: Completion using generic semifreeness off the diagonal
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is proved by using generic semifreeness to handle the diagonal and the removal-of-singularities procedure to remove all off-diagonal failures, while noting that stronger bounds would require an h-principle for semifree maps that is not yet available.

examples.name: Planar parabola
examples.note: The parabola is used only to make the definition concrete by showing a curve with no pair of parallel tangent lines.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker cites the planar parabola as a simple example and states that it has no parallel tangent lines, without deriving or checking this on the curve.

examples.name: Complex parabola z ↦ (z,z²)
examples.note: The complex analogue of the parabola illustrates a higher-dimensional totally nonparallel immersion and foreshadows the later quadratic viewpoint without supplying a necessary proof step.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker identifies the complex parabola and gives its coordinate form, but does not compute tangents or verify the totally nonparallel condition for it.

examples.name: Nonparallel loop on a torus in R³
examples.note: The sketched S¹ in R³, imagined as a loop on a torus, illustrates why leaving the plane can remove parallel tangent directions.
examples.engagement_reason: The torus loop is invoked pictorially as an imagined example with no two parallel tangent lines, but no construction or verification is carried out.

examples.name: The unresolved S² case
examples.note: In response to a question, S² is used as a small test case where configuration-space obstructions and possible restrictions of bilinear-map constructions give only partial information.
examples.engagement_reason: The S² case is discussed as unknown, with only vague comments about possible obstructions and constructions and no actual computation worked out.
